# Telegram fetcher PoC plan implementation audit

Audited plan: `.github/notes/Telegram fetcher, PoC implementation plan.md`.

Scope: fresh specification discrepancies found after `.github/reports/20260517T101916Z.md` and `.github/reports/20260517T101938Z.md`. Previously reported gaps are intentionally omitted.

Verification:
- `go test ./...` passes.
- `go vet ./...` fails, but that is a code-review issue tracked in `.github/reviews/20260601T055506Z.md`.

| Ref | Gap                                                            | Severity |
| --- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | -------- |
| R1  | [[#PEER FLOOD errors are not converted to user guidance]]      | Med      |
| R2  | [[#Fetch commands do not print the authorized account marker]] | Med      |
| R3  | [[#Auth status does not print the active proxy descriptor]]    | Med      |
| R4  | [[#UID cleanup misses quoted angle-wrapped values]]            | Low      |

## PEER FLOOD errors are not converted to user guidance

Plan §8 and §10 define RateGuard as the central handler for `PEER_FLOOD`, with fail-fast behavior and guidance. The implementation detects the error but returns it raw (`internal/telegram/rate.go:32-33`). Users receive the Telegram RPC error without the planned explanation of what happened or what to do next.

Fix: wrap `PEER_FLOOD` in an actionable error before returning it from RateGuard, while preserving the original error for `errors.Is` / Telegram error classification where useful.

## Fetch commands do not print the authorized account marker

Plan §10.1 includes `authorized as @<username> (id=<n>)` in the default lifecycle markers once auth settles. Existing-session fetches only log connection start and finish in `Client.Run` (`internal/telegram/api.go:86-113`); the only code that calls `auth.Client.Status` and logs the user is `Login` (`internal/telegram/auth.go:15-27`).

Impact: a user with multiple sessions or overridden `--session` paths can start a long fetch without seeing which Telegram account is active.

Fix: after `Client.Run` enters the callback, check `auth.Client.Status` once for commands that require authorization and emit the account marker before fetch work begins.

## Auth status does not print the active proxy descriptor

Plan §5.2 says `auth status` prints the active proxy descriptor as `type + host:port` without credentials. The command logs proxy data to stderr through the logger (`internal/command/telegram/auth.go:72-79`), but `PrintStatus` writes only session, authorization, DC, and user to stdout (`internal/telegram/auth.go:72-88`).

Impact: under `--quiet`, the proxy line disappears entirely, and scripts parsing `auth status` cannot see whether the run is direct or proxied.

Fix: add a credential-free proxy field to `Status` / `PrintStatus` and keep the stderr lifecycle log as secondary diagnostics.

## UID cleanup misses quoted angle-wrapped values

Plan §4.2 says UID normalization trims surrounding angle brackets, backticks, and quotes. `clean` strips angle brackets before quotes (`internal/telegram/uid/uid.go:95-101`), so an input copied as `"<https://t.me/telegram>"` becomes `<https://t.me/telegram>` after quote stripping and then fails URL parsing.

Impact: a note/shell round-trip form that the UID grammar is meant to tolerate is rejected.

Fix: normalize wrappers in a small loop, or strip quotes/backticks first and angle brackets second, until no wrapper remains.
